assignment_description:
Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune
⍞ Materials: Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune; Canadian Student Atlas or map of Nova Scotia

➜ INTRO
The years 1800-1900 were a momentous century for the history of Canada. It saw wars, rebellions, large waves of immigration, new and expanded settlements, exploration to the west and north, and Canada's Confederation of 1867, with more provinces and territories joining in the decades that followed. This course uses the narrative history The Story of Canada to provide an overview of the major events of the years 1800-1900, and a historical novel, a picture book, and a graphic novel to immerse students in three distinct moments in time: life in a fishing village on Nova Scotia's Chebucto Peninsula at the turn of the century, the life of Tecumseh, a Shawnee chief and British army ally in the War of 1812, and the Confederation Conferences of 1864, where the wheels of Canada's Confederacy were set in motion.
The first book, Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune, tells the story of a thirteen-year-old boy who risked his life to save drowning sailors from the wreck of the H.M.S. Tribune, off the coast of Nova Scotia. This historical fiction novel imagines a backstory for the boy only known to history as "Joe Cracker," and it paints a detailed picture of what life was like in a small fishing village around 1800.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.1-13 "Wisps of fog" - "all about the ocean."

• Who are the characters you meet as the novel opens? How are they related to each other? What are they like? You may choose to keep a character list with some notes.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resources: Canadian Student Atlas p.41 or any other map (including online) showing Nova Scotia
Locate Halifax and the Chebucto Peninsula. Does your map show Herring Cove?

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: Explore Herring Cove
Watch a video tour of Herring Cove with Senator Stan Kutcher. If you're short on time, feel free to skip 1:58-2:54. This whole video could be saved for another point in the course, but it touches on many themes of the novel, so it's definitely not a video to miss!
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Please read the header pages for this course that list the page numbers for the sensitive content you may wish to preview.

assignment_description:
The War of 1812
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada; atlas or map of Canada

➜ INTRO
In Joe and the Wreck of the Tribune, when Joe gets a tour of a Navy ship, the sailor Jacko tells him, "We's at war, you know." The British Navy had been engaged in naval conflicts for much of the eighteenth century and continued until peace was declared in 1815 after the Battle of Waterloo. One of Britain's last major battles before 1815 was the War of 1812, which involved Canada. Over the next few weeks, we will learn more about this war and why it was significant to Canada's history.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.78-81 "Britain and the United States" - "British North America would stay British."
p.92 and 94 (skip page 93) "A few years later" - "into the North."

➜ MAP WORK
Using a map of Ontario or Canada, locate Queenston Heights (near Niagara Falls), Detroit, Toronto (called York in 1812), Montreal, the St. Lawrence River, and the Great Lakes (Lake Ontario, Lake Erie, Lake Huron, Lake Michigan, and Lake Superior).

➜ VIEW
1\. Spend some time looking at this engraving of The Battle of Queenston Heights. The original engraving this one is based on is attributed to Captain James Dennis, who fought in the battle. The Americans (in blue) are crossing the Niagara River to Queenston (the right side) from Lewiston (the left side).
∞ Image Link: The Battle of Queenston Heights

2\. Visit Brock's Monument at Queenston Heights. You don't need to watch the whole video, but the drone footage provides an amazing view!
∞ Video Link: Canada's Most Imposing War Memorial

• IMPORTANT DATES
Tecumseh (1768-1813)
Sir Isaac Brock (1769–1812)
War of 1812 (1812)

• EXTRA HELPING
Watch a Heritage Minute about how the Grand River warriors helped to defeat the Americans at Queenston Heights after the death of General Brock.
∞ Video Link: Heritage Minutes: Queenston Heights

Tecumseh
⍞ Materials: Tecumseh

➜ INTRO
For the next three weeks, you will learn more about the life of Tecumseh, the Shawnee chief and warrior who allied with the British against the threat of invasion from the United States during the War of 1812. Tecumseh rallied other Indigenous peoples to side with him and had a vision of the ancestral lands being restored to First Nations tribes. He was not only an ally but was a personal friend of General Isaac Brock, who commanded the British troops in Upper Canada. Tecumseh played an integral part in protecting British North America from the American army.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.8-21 "Tecumseh was a renowned Shawnee" - "leader of the native peoples."

• What was Tecumseh's childhood like? What were some of the most formative events in his early years?
• The illustrations in this book are very vivid. Which ones stand out to you the most?
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Parents/teachers may wish to preview this picture book for more sensitive readers. Tecumseh lived through turbulent times, and the story does not omit the loss and violence of battles. Note, if combining: some illustrations may upset younger children.

The Métis & The Red River Settlement
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada; map or atlas of Canada that includes Manitoba

➜ INTRO
While the War of 1812 was at its height in Upper and Lower Canada to the east, a Scottish nobleman named Thomas Douglas, 5th Earl of Selkirk, was working to establish a colony on the prairies to the west, at the intersection of the Red and Assiniboine Rivers, near what is now Winnipeg. Selkirk had received a large grant of land from the Hudson Bay Company and wanted to create a farming community to support many poor Scottish highlanders and Irish peasants, who had lost their homes and livelihoods due to various upheavals at the end of the eighteenth century. This community, known as the Red River Settlement, would come to play a large role in the history of immigrants to the prairies, Métis peoples, the province of Manitoba, and the history of Canada as a nation.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.114-120 "In April 1806" - "cries of despair."

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
Look at a map or atlas of Manitoba (online is fine) and locate the Red River and Fort Douglas (present-day Winnipeg). Find the Hudson Bay on the same map so you can see the route the settlers took.
Compare this modern map to a map of the land given to Selkirk:
∞ Map Link: Map showing the Selkirk Land Grant in 1811

➜ VIEW
Learn more about elements of Métis culture, several of which are mentioned in today's reading: Métis sash, Red River cart, Red River jig, Métis flag, cuisine, beadwork, and fiddle. Follow the "Learn More" links under each heading.
∞ Link: Symbols of Métis Culture

• IMPORTANT DATES
First settlers arrive at the Selkirk settlement in Red River (1811)

Rebellion and Reform
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ RECAP
What did you read about immigration to Canada in the early nineteenth century?

➜ INTRO
How do we choose our government in Canada and in our provinces and territories? Do you think it has always been this way in Canada? Today we will learn about frustrations towards the government in the 1830s, which would eventually lead to the form of government we recognize today.

➜ VIEW
Remember that in 1791, the large Province of Quebec was divided into Upper and Lower Canada, roughly corresponding to the provinces of Ontario and Quebec today.
∞ Link: Upper & Lower Canada in 1791

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS

• What changes did William Lyon Mackenzie want to see in Upper Canada?

• EXTRA HELPING

∞ Image Link: Cornelius Krieghoff at the National Gallery of Canada

• IMPORTANT DATES
Louis-Joseph Papineau (1786-1871)
William Lyon Mackenzie (1795–1861)

⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS

• EXTRA HELPING
Watch two Heritage Minutes about the reforms that were brought about by the rebellions in the 1830s:
∞ Video Link: Heritage Minutes: Baldwin & LaFontaine (Parents may wish to preview this one for younger students since it does dramatize a violent scene.)
∞ Video Link: Heritage Minutes: Responsible Government
Queen Victoria voices her approval of the reforms.

The sailing ship Marco Polo met its fate in a storm off the coast of Cavendish, and its running aground was witnessed by a young L.M. Montgomery, who later wrote an essay about it for the Montreal Daily Witness (March 5, 1891). You can read her essay here.
∞ PDF Link: The Wreck of the Marco Polo

The Loxleys and Confederation
⍞ Materials: The Loxleys and Confederation

➜ INTRO
For the next four weeks, you will read an account of the years leading up to Confederation through the eyes of a fictional family, the Loxleys, in the graphic novel The Loxleys and Confederation. Look for historical figures and places (and a piece of artwork!) that you have been learning about this year.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Beginning with the Prologue on page 9, read for about 20 minutes, leaving time for narration. This should allow enough time to read through The Loxleys and Confederation over four weeks. Since the time to read graphic novels varies, some students will also have time to read Lillian's 1867 Diary at the end, while others can save that for afternoon occupations or an extra helping.

Confederation
⍞ Materials: The Story of Canada

➜ INTRO
In 1867, Canada looked very different from what it does today. It consisted of four provinces: Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Quebec, and Ontario. How and when did other provinces join Confederation? And what did this mean for the Indigenous peoples in the west and north who had lived on the land for centuries?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.166-172 "July 1, 1867" - "Riel was an exile."

• Why did some colonies choose not to join in 1867?
• What were some effects of Canada's purchase of Rupert's Land from the Hudson's Bay Company?

➜ VIEW
Look at the map of Rupert's Land, the land sold to Canada by the Hudson's Bay Company in 1869. Which provinces and territories now exist on land that was once part of Rupert's Land?
∞ Map Link: Rupert's Land

• EXTRA HELPING
Listen to a performance of the Anti-Confederation Song, recorded only ten years after Newfoundland eventually joined Canada in 1949.
∞ Music Link: Alan Mills - Anti-Confederation Song (1959)

• IMPORTANT DATES
Louis Riel (1844-1885)
Manitoba joins Canada (1870)

• EXTRA HELPING
Louis Riel was tried and hanged for high treason in 1885, but history has come to regard him as, instead, one of the fathers of Confederation - the father of Manitoba. In 2024, Manitoba formally acknowledged him as their first Premier.
∞ Article Link: Riel formally recognized as first Premier

Did you notice the young boy front and centre in the photograph of the last spike being hammered into the railway at Craigellachie, BC? Learn more about him.
∞ Article Link: The Kid in the Last Spike

John A. Macdonald's wife Agnes had a unique way of riding the train! See another photograph of her from 1887.
∞ Article Link: History Corner -1887- Lady Agnes Macdonald
